title: Ovaries Might Take on an Immune Function After Menopause
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementHealthOvaries Might Take on an Immune Function After MenopauseThe reproductive organs might have hidden role JCBy Jake Currie3:30 PM CDT on July 1, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyWhen it comes to aging, some people like to say your memory goes first. In reality, it’s the ovaries. Among all the organs in the human body, the ovaries are the earliest to show signs of decline, closing up shop around the age of 52. While scientists long believed they simply became inert after exhausting their egg reserves, that may not be the case. According to new research published in Molecular Human Reproduction, ovaries may take on a second, immune-related job after menopause.Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Scientists Keep Finding Cocaine Sharks Off the Coast of BrazilShareNextStayReproductive biologists led by Francesca Duncan of Northwestern University made the discovery after studying the ovaries of mice. Like humans, mouse ovaries also go quiet, typically around the two-year mark (although they don’t experience the same decline in estrogen humans do). To investigate how their reproductive organs change, the team removed ovaries from young mice, middle-aged mice, and post-reproductive mice. They sequenced the RNA from one ovary of each mouse while examining the other for structural tissue and cell changes. Read more: “The Evolutionary Mystery of Menopause”Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisement
